title: ComfyUI Tutorial Series Ep 63: API Nodes - Run Nano Banana, GPT-5 & Seedream 4
description:
In this episode, we take a deep look at API Nodes in ComfyUI. These are special nodes that connect workflows to external AI services like Google Gemini (Nano Banana), GPT-5, and Seedream 4. You will learn how to use credits, run paid models without local installs, and set up workflows for image editing, text-to-image generation, batch inputs, and LLM prompt generation.

By the end of this tutorial, you will know:
- What API Nodes are and how they connect ComfyUI to external AI models
- The advantages and limitations of running models via API instead of locally
- How to top up credits and manage paid model usage
- How to build workflows with Nano Banana (Gemini 2.5 Flash Image) for edits and text-to-image
- How to generate large 2K and 4K images with Seedream 4
- How to use GPT-5 Nano inside ComfyUI for prompt generation and automation
- Tips for chaining multiple images, using batch nodes, and comparing results

This tutorial is perfect if you want to unlock more models in ComfyUI with
transcript: Welcome to episode 63 of our Comfy UI Tutorial series.  API nodes in Comfy UI are special nodes  that let you connect your workflow to outside AI services,  like OpenAI, Stability AI, or other model providers  through their APIs.  Instead of running everything locally on your computer,  you can send a request to those services,  get back the generated image, text, or data,  and then continue working with it inside Comfy UI  just like any other node.  Basically, they act as bridges between Comfy UI  and external AI models you don't have installed locally.  Advantage is, API nodes give you access to powerful models  like GPT-5, Flux Pro, or Nano Banana  without needing downloads or high-end hardware,  and they are always up to date.  Disadvantages, they require internet and paid keys,  can be slower, cost money over time,  and offer less control compared to running models locally.  Let's go to the Comfy UI menu and choose Settings.  Here we have an option called User that we can click  and now it lets us sign in or sign up for a Comfy UI account.  So let's click on the button,  and now you can sign in with email or other options,  or click Sign Up to create an account.  For me, it's easier to just sign up with Google,  so I will do that.  Now you can see I am logged in because there is a logout option.  Let's close this window,  and now on the top right we have an icon with more options.  As you probably know, most APIs that use paid models need credits.  I already had some credits here,  but let's click top up to get more credits so we can test different models.  I think I will choose $10 for now,  and then click the Buy Now button.  You can pay with a card or with Google Pay,  click Pay, and after a few seconds you should get a message saying the purchase was successful.  Now I can go back to Comfy UI,  and if I go to User, you can see I have those $10 extra added to my credits.  If you click on User Settings, you are back in the menu where you can log out.  Let's see how we can spend some of those credits.  If you go to Browser Templates,  Comfy UI already offers a lot of templates for API nodes.  For example, it has image generation workflows, video workflows,  a few for 3D, some for audio, and some for LLMs like ChatGPT and Gemini.  But I will show you the ones I use most often since they are cheaper,  because some models like Video Generation cost quite a lot.  If we go to the Comfy.org website,  on the top we have a Resources menu,  and from there we can click on Docs.  If we scroll down to Tutorials,  there is one option for API nodes,  and if we click on the arrow, we can find the Pricing option.  Here you can see how much it costs to use each of those models.  As you can see, there are paid models like Flux Pro,  which wasn't released for free since we only got Flux Dev.  Some models are from Bite Dance for Image and Video.  Kling AI also has many models for Image and Video,  and most of the popular paid models can probably be found here.  You just need to decide which ones are most useful for your work and worth the money.  Enough talking, let's create something.  If you click on the plus sign at the top,  you can create a new blank workflow,  and with X you can close it.  I already have one blank workflow open,  so let's double click on the Canvas and type Google.  As you can see, it starts showing API nodes,  so you can easily recognize which ones are API.  I will select the Google Gemini Image.  You probably know this model as Nano Banana,  but the official name is Gemini 2.5 Flash Image.  These special nodes have a dollar sign on top  to notify you that they cost money.  In this case, it costs 3 cents for 1000 tokens,  which basically means each image generated with it costs 3 cents.  So with $3 you can generate 100 images.  Now we just need an input and an output to make it work.  Double click and search for a load image node,  then connect that to the image.  Since Nano Banana can edit images,  if you don't connect an image, it will generate a new one instead of editing.  Then we need to save the result.  So on the output, we connect a save image node.  That's the workflow.  An image goes in, and the AI model outputs a new image  with the changes you asked for.  Let's click the button and upload an image.  I'll go with this bunny.  For the prompt, we give instructions just like we did with Quen  edit and flux context.  Let's ask for a side view of this cartoon bunny.  Now we can run the workflow,  and the information is sent to the cloud on Google's server.  In a few seconds, you get the result back.  It's not using your computer's processing power  because you're paying with credits to use their server.  And now I have three cents less in my account.  We can do something similar with Quen edit for free,  but you need a stronger Nvidia card.  Nano Banana can sometimes be a bit smarter than Quen edit,  especially for realistic images.  For example, I can load this photo of a woman  and change the prompt to put her in a red dress,  walking on a street in Paris at night.  After running it, in a few seconds, we get the result.  For things you can't do with free models,  it's worth trying API nodes with different models,  depending on the project.  It took about 13 seconds to generate,  so the data was sent to the server, processed, and then returned.  While it's working, you can do something else.  Now let's see if we can turn this woman into a cartoon.  I'll add some more details in the prompt,  and now we have a cartoon version of her.  You can also add multiple images.  For example, I can duplicate the load image node,  then add a batch images node.  We connect the first image and the second image to that batch node,  and then connect it to the Google Gemini node.  This works like uploading two images into Google Gemini or ChatGPT,  and then prompting together.  You can also chain multiple batch nodes to add more.  Let's add this man as the second image,  or maybe I'll put him first.  The order of the prompt doesn't matter,  but what matters is the ratio.  If the first image is square, the result will be square.  If the first image is portrait,  the result will also follow a similar ratio,  so keep that in mind.  For the prompt, let's create a selfie of these two in the jungle.  In this first seed, it looks like the woman is hiding behind the man.  Let's run it again for a different seed.  Now the result looks much better,  and the man and woman are quite consistent.  Let's try something else,  like adding my logo instead of the woman. I got this one, only the prompt exactly how I asked.  Now you can copy and paste it where you need,  or even connect this to other workflows.  For example, I can get a prompt for an image. And now I get a long detailed prompt that works great with other models like Flux or Quenn  that support long descriptive prompts.  You can copy these nodes into another workflow,  or save it as a template and import it into another workflow.  Let's say I open a Nunchaku Quenn workflow from episode 62. If I run this, I get something like this.  The result is quite similar, which means it did its job right.  Let's try with a different image.  I will try with this photo of a woman,  but this time I will prompt that I want the woman to have a green t-shirt, not red.  So basically we get an adapted prompt for that woman.
